reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo noted that the use of _get_random_bytes() in this patch would break kmalloc_nolock(), suggesting a replacement with prandom_u32_state(). Additionally, he questioned the necessity of random eviction, unlike multi-memcg percpu charge cache.
reviews.raw_body:
It would break kmalloc_nolock() because _get_random_bytes() uses a 
spinlock. perhaps prandom_u32_state() should be sufficient in this case.

Is there a reason why it uses random eviction, unlike multi-memcg percpu 
charge cache?

Otherwise LGTM!

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that refill_stock() also uses get_random_u32_below(), agreed to fix it separately and investigate using prandom_u32_state()
reviews.raw_body:
Oh I didn't know and actually we are already using get_random_u32_below() in
refill_stock(). So, it need fixing as well. That would be a separate patch.

I will explore prandom_u32_state().

reviews.author: Joshua Hahn
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the problem of random eviction in objcg stock can be eliminated by pushing memcg stock to page_counter, and suggested abstracting or pushing down objcg stock to a finer granularity slot such as per-X
reviews.raw_body:
Hello Harry and Shakeel!

I just wanted to note that my work to push the memcg stock to page_counter [1]
actually gets rid of the random eviction and also the get_random_u32_below.
So the problem will no longer exist, at least for memcg/page_counter stock.

I was also thinking whether there was any way we can abstract or push down the
objcg stock to a different per_X slot (with finer granularity than per-node)
so we don't do random evictions, but it doesn't seem like there's a natural
point to do this from a quick glance.

The fix for get_random_32_below to use prandom_u32_state seems quite simple
though, so if either of you would like to send that in as a small hotfix
it shouldn't be a big deal to rebase on top of that instead too.

(Sorry for joining the conversation late too. I'll take a closer look at this
entire series tomorrow as well).

Thank you both. Have a great day!
Joshua

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410210742.550489-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/

2026-05-20.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) implementation, specifically related to object cgroups (objcgs). The issue arises from the conversion of objcg to be per-memcg and per-node type, which causes thrashing on workloads where threads of the same memcg run on different NUMA nodes. To resolve this, the patch introduces multi-slot support for obj_stock_pcp, allowing multiple cached objcg variants of one memcg to coexist without forcing a drain. This is achieved by turning nr_bytes and cached_objcg into arrays, scanning all slots during consume/refill/account operations, and evicting a random slot only when full.

reviews.author: Harry Yoo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Harry Yoo noted that SLUB had a similar issue of thrashing due to spinning on different NUMA nodes, and suggested using prandom if spinning is not allowed when shuffling slab freelist
sentiment_signals: Requested a comparison with a similar issue in SLUB
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks!

FYI, SLUB had a similar issue that was recently fixed:
commit a1e244a9f1778 ("mm/slab: use prandom if !allow_spin").

It uses prandom if spinning is not allowed when shuffling slab freelist.

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

2026-05-21.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance regression in the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) implementation, specifically related to object cgroups (objcgs). The issue arises from the conversion of objcg to be per-memcg and per-node type, which causes thrashing on workloads where threads of the same memcg run on different NUMA nodes. To resolve this, the patch introduces multi-slot support for obj_stock_pcp, allowing multiple cached objcg variants of one memcg to coexist without forcing a drain. This is achieved by turning nr_bytes and cached_objcg into arrays, scanning all slots during consume/refill/account operations, and evicting a random slot only when full.
